data.title: Small Surge, Micro Mighty
data.description:
By Brandon Nelson, CFA

Strong earnings and a US Federal Reserve interest rate cut fueled healthy equity performance during the third quarter. Even September returns were positive despite consensus concerns relating to typical seasonal weakness. Although large caps still lead small caps year-to-date by 421 basis points (as measured by the Russell 2000 Index versus the Russell 1000 Index),1 small caps outperformed for the quarter, rising an impressive 12.39% and exceeding large-cap performance by 440 basis points.

Microcaps, the smallest of the small, were up a whopping 17.03% for the quarter, as measured by the Russell Microcap Index. Microcaps have outperformed small caps and mid caps for four consecutive months and are actually leading all equity asset classes year-to-date (even large caps!). Even so, they still trade at a significant discount to all equity asset classes when looking at 2026 consensus P/E ratios.

Both Calamos Timpani Small Cap Growth Fund (CTSIX) and Calamos Timpani SMID Growth Fund (CTIGX) had excellent quarters. Each fund outperformed its respective benchmark and Morningstar category average during the quarter, extending year-to-date gains.

CTSIX and CTIGX: Strong Outperformance in 2025 Versus Benchmarks and Peers

As shown below, the September quarter was extremely difficult for most active small growth and SMID growth asset managers. For instance, only 15.4% of small growth managers and 5.1% of SMID growth managers outperformed their comparable market benchmark, which we believe makes the strength of the Calamos Timpani funds that much more impressive.

So what’s been driving our strong performance? It’s been a combination of our investment style being embraced by the market (our focus on fundamental momentum—sustainably fast and underestimated growth) and successful stock picking within that framework, including our exposure to microcaps.

While most of our stock picks are one-off situations, we’ve also identified compelling niche themes relating to AI infrastructure, online gambling, education, advanced military technology, and securing supply chains of critical materials. We believe these themes have room to run and our ability to find idiosyncratic opportunities will continue.

As our performance has shown, we don’t need asset class strength to put points on the board. However, asset class strength could provide another way for our funds to win. We believe microcaps, small caps, and mid caps may be poised for a performance rebound relative to large caps. Small caps have lagged for over eight years, marking the longest losing streak on record and making their valuations relative to large caps look quite stretched (10th percentile). Importantly, a projected earnings acceleration for small caps (+20% in 2026) and additional Fed rate cuts could act as catalysts to trigger valuation multiple expansion.2

Recent small-cap outperformance may have marked the end of the losing streak. Historically, the first year of a new small-cap winning streak results in an average of 2,700 basis points of small-cap outperformance relative to large caps, and the best quarter for small caps has tended to be the fourth quarter.

Asset allocators, I would pay attention.

Footnotes

1 For the year to date ending 9/30/2025, the Russell 1000 Index gained 14.60% and the Russell 2000 Index gained 10.39%.

2 Relative valuation of small caps to large caps is in the 10th percentile as of September 30, 2025; lower percentiles indicate more favorable relative valuations for small caps. Source: Jefferies, using FactSet, FTSE Russell and Jefferies. Jefferies estimates for year-over-year expected earnings growth % is annual as of September 15, 2025, using FactSet, Standard and Poor’s and Jefferies.

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: S&P Global Services PMI: Growth Softened Slightly In September
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The September U.S. Services Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) from S&P Global came in at 54.2 ,down slightly from 54.5 in August. The reading marks the 32nd consecutive month of expansion and was higher than the forecast of 53.9.

From the latest press release, Chris Williamson, Chief Business Economist at S&P Global Market Intelligence, said:

“Service sector growth softened slightly in September but remained strong enough to round off an impressive performance over the third quarter a whole. Combined with sustained growth in the manufacturing sector, the expansion of service sector activity is indicative of robust third quarter annualized GDP growth of around 2.5%.

“Growth is being fueled principally by rising financial services and tech sector activity, though we are also seeing more signs of improving demand for consumer-facing services such as leisure and recreation, likely linked in part to lower interest rates. Lower borrowing costs have also fed through to a broad based improvement in business optimism about the outlook for the next 12 months.

“Disappointingly, the improvement in business optimism failed to spur more jobs growth, with hiring almost stalling in a sign of further labor market malaise as companies often focused on running more efficiently amid uncertain trading conditions.

“A further ongoing source of concern from the surveys are heightened cost pressures which survey respondents have attributed to tariffs. Input costs rose sharply again in September as import levies were seen to have again fed through from goods to services. However, rates charged for services rose at the slowest rate for five months in a welcome sign that some of these tariff price pressures in supply chains are starting to moderate.

Background on the S&P Global US Services PMI

The S&P Global US Services PMI™ measures the activity level of purchasing managers in the services sector through a questionnaire of ~400 service sector companies. The sectors covered include consumer (excluding retail), transportation, information, communication, finance, insurance, real estate, and business services.

The S&P Services PMI is a diffusion index, meaning that a reading above 50 indicates expansion in the sector compared to the previous month and a reading below 50 indicates contraction.

Here is a snapshot of the series since mid-2012.

Here is an overlay with the equivalent PMI survey conducted by the Institute for Supply Management, which it refers to as "non-manufacturing" (see our full article on this series here). Over its history, the ISM metric has been significantly the more volatile of the two.

The next chart uses a three-month moving average of the two rather volatile series to facilitate our understanding of the current trend.

S&P Global PMI Comparison: Manufacturing vs. Services

The next chart offers a comparison between the two Purchasing Managers' Indexes provided by S&P Global. See the latest update on the Manufacturing PMI here.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: TQQQ: An Alpha Opportunity
data.description:
There are two strong arguments for owning a tech-focused leveraged ETF product such as the ProShares UltraPro QQQ (NASDAQ:TQQQ): 1) AI spending is set to accelerate in the years ahead, which will benefit companies with considerable exposure to this specific sector of the U.S. economy, especially Magnificent 7 firms, and 2) the recent flurry of deals affecting the neocloud businesses, including companies that run GPU-as-a-Service business models such as CoreWeave (CRWV) and Nebius Group (NBIS), highlights that the upcoming third-quarter earnings season could create alpha for TQQQ investors.

ChatGPT creator OpenAI is on a massive spending spree in order to prepare the business for the age of super intelligence, which highlights sustained growth momentum for tech companies as well. The ProShares UltraPro QQQ could be a potentially lucrative investment product for investors that want to use the leveraged ETF as a tactical asset allocation instrument in preparation for a strong third-quarter earnings season.

Previous Rating

In my last review of the ProShares UltraPro QQQ -- Material Outperformance Potential -- I highlighted the potential of artificial intelligence as a major growth catalyst for the ETF's net asset value growth: since then, shares have increased 27.4% in value, and more upside may lurk just around the corner as companies get ready to open their books for Q3. Recently, we have seen multi-billion-dollar deals in the Data Center industry and deal sizes are starting to get really big. This creates a favorable backdrop for investors that want to invest, in anticipation of a strong earnings season, in the tech sector.

Increasing Concentration Of Magnificent 7 Stocks, Strong Tech Earnings Season Ahead

The ProShares UltraPro QQQ seeks to 3x the daily performance of the Nasdaq-100 Index and is therefore a leveraged ETF product with above-average levels of volatility. However, the ProShares UltraPro QQQ could be a potentially suitable investment product for investors that expect top results from the country's leading suite of technology companies.

The main reason why I like the ProShares UltraPro QQQ is because the ETF is a directional bet on the technology industry, and especially on companies that have considerable AI exposure, such as the Magnificent 7. This 'mag-7' group of stocks includes Nvidia (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Tesla (TSLA), Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG) and Meta Platforms (META).

The ProShares UltraPro QQQ has been heavily concentrated in Magnificent 7 stocks... all of which have significant exposure to the theme of artificial intelligence. Since my last coverage on the ProShares UltraPro QQQ, however, the ETF has seen a material increase in the concentration of Magnificent 7 stocks, due to the broad rise in market valuations.

A number of artificial intelligence stocks have seen record highs lately, including Nvidia, which is the world’s largest AI GPU maker, and Alphabet, which recently received a favorable Chrome ruling from a U.S. federal court.

Nvidia currently has a portfolio rating of 9.2%, compared to a 5.8% portfolio share in my last coverage in June. Microsoft and Apple, the ETF’s second- and third-largest holdings, increased their allocations by 3.2 PP and 2.6 PP respectively. TQQQ's top ten holdings now represent 50.8% of all the ETF’s investments, compared to just 32.2% previously. The Magnificent 7's share has gone up to 39.8%, showing an increase of 14.6 PP since my last coverage.

The ProShares UltraPro QQQ has therefore been a major beneficiary of soaring AI spending in the Data Center business, but recent deals indicate that we are still very much at the beginning of a new age of superintelligence... creating upside revaluation potential as the total addressable market expands.

The AI data center industry has seen major deals recently, which included a $17.4B (+$2.0B deal upside) agreement between Nebius and Microsoft for the provision of cloud compute -- Microsoft Deal Changes Everything -- as well as a $6.3B deal between Nvidia and CoreWeave. Additionally, Nvidia and OpenAI agreed on a $100B investment deal -- which is part of OpenAI's grand plan to build out its Data Center footprint to the tune of 850B over the next several years.

According to business consulting company McKinsey, global Data Center capacity demand is set for explosive growth until the end of the decade, and this demand growth is chiefly driven by AI workloads. These workloads are expected to see a factor increase of 3.5X between FY 2025 and FY 2030, and create the backdrop for sustained growth in the Magnificent 7 investment category.

With deal sizes in the Data Center industry increasing rapidly, strong results for Magnificent 7 companies can be expected for the upcoming earnings season... which starts later this month. Investors that want to play the potential upside that comes with better-than-expected earnings reports for Q3 '25, may want to consider buying into the ProShares UltraPro QQQ.

TQQQ: Strong Performance History

The ProShares UltraPro QQQ currently has a net asset value of $106.09/share, and the ETF is trading at a small NAV premium of 0.04%. For comparison, the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) is trading at a 0.01% premium and offers investors essentially the same Nasdaq-100 exposure, just without the added kicker of leverage that the TQQQ is using. I covered the QQQ here and continue to recommend the ETF as a strong buy option, even at all-time highs, for investors that are a little more risk-averse: A Growth ETF With An AI Catalyst.

The ProShares UltraPro QQQ has significantly outperformed the Invesco QQQ Trust, which is due to the leveraged nature of the TQQQ and the fact that the tech sector has delivered very strong returns, especially since the advent of ChatGPT in 2022. The ProShares UltraPro QQQ, in the context of recent AI deals, has continual outperformance potential, in my opinion.

Risks with TQQQ

A material risk factor for investors investing in a leveraged 3x investment product is volatility drag which is due to the daily rebalancing of an ETF’s leverage position. Volatility drag means investors may lose out on returns in an exceptionally volatile market -- as the TQQQ seeks to 3x the daily performance of the Nasdaq- 100 -- which may lead to underperformance long term. A leveraged ETF magnifies the volatility of the underlying index and is therefore mainly suitable for investors seeking a short-term asset allocation tool. The risks associated with leveraged ETFs need to be clearly understood and include the potential for significant capital loss.

Final thoughts

The age of superintelligence is upon us, and the ProShares UltraPro QQQ could be a potentially interesting investment product for investors that have a very high risk tolerance and are willing to accept a high degree of volatility in their portfolios.

Given the progressively increasing deal sizes for companies that run GPU-as-a-Service business models, and OpenAI's massive $850B spending spree in order to build out its Data Center footprint, there is a strong likelihood, in my opinion, that the third-quarter earnings season is going to be very strong for tech companies.

Nvidia is trading near all-time highs and has recently struck major deals with neocloud businesses like CoreWeave as well as OpenAI. AI-spending is set to accelerate further, in my opinion, potentially leading to more deals benefiting the Magnificent 7 companies. I believe TQQQ could be a major winner in the upcoming earnings season, and I confirm my strong buy rating.

data.title: European Bank Stocks Extend Rally In Q3, Surpassing U.S., Asia Rivals
data.description:
European banks extended their stock rally in the third quarter, surging ahead of US and Asian counterparts.

The S&P Europe BMI Banks index outperformed US and APAC bank indexes, rising 14.71% in the three-month period. The index has risen 73.89% year to date, versus a 25.48% increase for the US S&P 500 Bank index and a 22.25% rise for the S&P Pan Asia BMI Banks index, S&P Global Market Intelligence data shows.

European banking stocks have been propelled by buoyant quarterly profits, supported by strong noninterest income that helped offset the impact of declining interest rates on lending income. Banks also benefited from a surge in trading income amid heightened market volatility.

Improved profitability and robust capital buffers position mean European banks are well placed to absorb potentially higher credit losses due to rising trade tensions, according to the European Banking Authority's latest EU-wide bank stress test. This could lead to higher dividends and buybacks by the region's banks.

Bank M&A activity in Europe also continues to gather steam. Recent transactions include Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena SpA (OTCPK:BMDPF) gaining control of Italian peer Mediobanca Banca di Credito Finanziario SpA (OTCPK:MDIBF)(OTCPK:MDIBY) and Spanish group Banco Santander SA's (SAN) £2.65 billion deal to acquire UK-based TSB Banking Group PLC from Banco de Sabadell SA.

Spain's four largest banks were also among the best-performing European banking stocks during the quarter.

Santander logged a total return of 26.3%, while Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria SA (BBVA) and Sabadell registered gains of 25.2% and 24.7%, respectively. CaixaBank SA (OTCPK:CIXPF)(OTCPK:CAIXY) had a total return of 21.6%, Market Intelligence data shows.

Strong economic growth in Spain is expected to fuel growth and further bolster banks' profitability rebound as they navigate challenging geopolitical and global trade tensions.

Two Italian banks also made the top 15 list. Banco BPM SpA (OTCPK:BNCZF), which avoided a takeover by larger peer UniCredit SpA (OTCPK:UNCFF)(OTCPK:UNCRY), had a total return of 28.4%. BPER Banca SpA (OTCPK:BPXXY), which bought Banca Popolare di Sondrio SpA, recorded a 22.3% gain.

Greece-based CrediaBank SA was the best-performing European bank stock in the third quarter, with a total return of 99.8%. The company, formerly known as Attica Bank, recently signed a put option agreement to acquire a 70.03% stake in HSBC Holdings PLC's (HSBC) Malta unit for €200 million. Larger domestic peer Piraeus Financial Holdings SA (OTCPK:BPIRF)(OTCPK:BPIRY) delivered gains of 22.6% as it reported a higher-than-expected net interest income (NII) result for the second quarter.

Sweden's TF Bank AB (publ) ranked second on the list. The digital bank, which began trading on the Nasdaq Stockholm in July following a 3:1 stock split, plans to change its corporate name to Avarda Bank AB.

QNB Bank AS, the Turkish unit of Qatar National Bank QPSC, rounded out the top three, with a total return of 51.7%.

Poland-based Bank Polska Kasa Opieki SA (OTCPK:BKPKF) and Bank Handlowy w Warszawie SAregistered negative shareholder returns of 5.6% and 4.7%, respectively. Shares in the country's banks are yet to rebound after plummeting Aug. 22, following government proposals to increase the corporate income tax rate for banks to help fund defense and healthcare.

Some savings banks in Norway also took a hit during the quarter, with SpareBank 1 Nord-Norge (OTCPK:SPXXF), SpareBank 1 Sør-Norge ASA (OTCPK:SBRKF) and SpareBank 1 Østlandet (OTCPK:SPRBF) registering negative total returns ranging from 3.3% to 4.4%. DNB Bank ASA (OTCPK:DNBBF)(OTCPK:DNBBY), Norway's largest bank, logged a negative return of 2.5%. The Norges Bank in September delivered a quarter-percentage-point cut in interest rates to 4%, citing an uncertain economic outlook, but projected only one rate cut per year in the coming three years.

Also on the list are Turkey-based Akbank TAS (OTCQX:AKBTY)and Türkiye Vakiflar Bankasi TAO (OTC:TKYVY), with respective negative returns of 8.1% and 3.3%, respectively. The Turkish BIST Banks Index tumbled in September after a court ordered the removal of the Istanbul head of the main opposition Republican People's Party. The ruling dealt a fresh judicial setback to opponents of President Recep Tayyip Erdoğan, news outlets including Reuters and Bloomberg News wrote.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Neo-Clouds With Gold AI Linings
data.description:
By Yan Taw (YT) Boon

A new breed of smaller cloud service providers is offering high-performance AI computing at a fraction of what Amazon (AMZN) and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) charge.

Last month, U.S. President Donald Trump brought together 33 of Silicon Valley’s most influential leaders to address a burgeoning challenge: How to meet insatiable demand for data-processing power in the age of artificial intelligence?

It’s a question worthy of such a summit. Even cloud service giants like Amazon, Google and Microsoft (MSFT) are hitting data-processing bottlenecks. While those so-called hyperscalers continue to invest heavily in custom chips, power systems and next-generation data center architecture, no single player can meet the scale and complexity of AI’s demands alone. Instead, tech’s titans are now looking to forge strategic partnerships with a new breed of “neo-cloud” providers.

Unlike the hyperscalers, which offer a broad range of cloud solutions, neo-cloud providers simply rent raw computing power for AI and advanced applications - a business model known as “GPUs-as-a-service.”

Instead of relying on massive, centralized data centers, these scrappy operators deploy their data-crunching power across thousands of smaller centers or regional hubs, increasing the flexibility and resilience of their networks - not unlike high-frequency traders who aim to shave off precious milliseconds by placing their servers closer to securities exchanges.

By partnering with neo-cloud newcomers, such as Nebius (NBIS) and CoreWeave (CRWV), behemoths like Microsoft aim to handle daunting AI workloads at increasing scale. For example, Microsoft recently signed a deal with Nebius - worth up to $20 billion over five years - under which Microsoft will lease computing power from Nebius’ new data center in Vineland, New Jersey.1

Meanwhile, leading AI chipmaker Nvidia (NVDA) has taken a significant equity stake in CoreWeave, worth roughly $3 billion at the end of June, and has also agreed to purchase up to $6.3 billion in unsold data-processing capacity from the company through the spring of 2032.2 (Nvidia is a shareholder in Nebius as well.)

For smaller enterprises, we believe neo-clouds have the potential to lower the barriers to entry for high-performance AI and advanced analytics. Thanks to their streamlined operations, neo-cloud providers can rent their GPUs at an hourly rate between two and seven times lower than what hyperscalers typically charge,3 potentially allowing young businesses to get on their feet without having to make large upfront investments or long-term commitments in cutting-edge technology.

Furthermore, neo-clouds can offer industry-specific data-processing power, potentially giving them an additional edge over the hyperscalers. For example, Nebius offers cloud services expressly designed to support the e-commerce industry - a big reason it recently landed Shopify (SHOP) as a client.4

Yet the benefits of neo-clouds extend well beyond technical agility, in our view. Amid broader trends toward de-globalization and regional data sovereignty, we believe newer cloud service players may be better positioned than the hyperscalers - and perhaps more trusted - to comply with local data governance and environmental mandates, including Europe’s General Data Protection Regulation, Artificial Intelligence Act, and Digital Operation Resilience Act. CoreWeave, for one, aims to build advanced AI “factories” in the UK powered by renewable energy and advanced cooling technology to limit water consumption.5

Over the next five years, the total addressable GPU-as-a-service market is expected to grow at 35% a year, reaching $260 billion by 2030.6 We will be keeping an eye on those providers with strong relationships with chipmakers and hyperscalers, as well as those with differentiated software stacks catering to specific industries. For thematic tech investors, we believe the forecast calls for a strong chance of neo-clouds.

1“Microsoft taps Nebius to supply up to $20bn of AI computing power,” Financial Times, September 9, 2025;

2“CoreWeave’s stock rallies on disclosure of $6.3 billion order from Nvidia,” CNBC, September 15, 2025;

3“What is a Neocloud? The Rise of GPU-only Clouds,” Thunder Compute, April 25, 2025;

4“Augmented Commerce: Machine Learning at Shopify,” July 4, 2025;

5CoreWeave Announces £1.5 Billion Commitment to Power UK AI Innovation and Growth Through Sustainable Computing, September 16, 2025;

6Nebius Group N.V., December 31, 2024.

This material is provided for informational and educational purposes only and nothing herein constitutes investment, legal, accounting or tax advice. This material is general in nature and is not directed to any category of investors and should not be regarded as individualized, a recommendation, investment advice or a suggestion to engage in or refrain from any investment-related course of action. Information is obtained from sources deemed reliable, but there is no representation or warranty as to its accuracy, completeness or reliability. All information is current as of the date of this material and is subject to change without notice. The firm, its employees and advisory accounts may hold positions of any companies discussed. Any views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of the firm as a whole. Neuberger Berman products and services may not be available in all jurisdictions or to all client types.

The views expressed herein include those of Neuberger Berman’s Asset Allocation Committee, which comprises professionals across multiple disciplines, including equity and fixed income strategists and portfolio managers. The Asset Allocation Committee reviews and sets long-term asset allocation models, establishes preferred near-term tactical asset class allocations and, upon request, reviews asset allocations for large, diversified mandates. Tactical asset allocation views are based on a hypothetical reference portfolio. data.title: Toll Brothers: Sturdy With Reasonable Valuation, But Upside Potential Is Limited
data.description:
Four months after my last article on Toll Brothers, Inc. (NYSE:TOL), the stock price has already surged by over 30% and justified my buy rating. This should not be surprising considering its robust performance and growth prospects when I last covered it. With its well-positioned business operations and valuation, TOL still trades at a reasonable level. Technicals still present bullish signals despite the recent dip and profit-taking. However, upside appears to be nearing its limit, so caution is crucial for those who wish to add more position today.

Why TOL Is Still Well-Positioned

Despite its established position and vast domestic market coverage, TOL is not insulated from macroeconomic risks. Inflation remains stubborn after picking up its momentum from 2.4% to 2.7% and to 2.9%. This may be exacerbated by tariff woes as it also imports construction materials and homebuilding products. This can lead to more cost pressures, which may prompt it to increase its prices. Despite this, TOL appears well-positioned due to several factors.

1. Its target market is more resistant and resilient to inflation. I’ve already mentioned several times that TOL’s primary customers come from upper-income households and even high-net-worth individuals or HNWIs. Basically, it builds luxury properties for affluent individuals. These homebuyers are less sensitive to price changes due to their above-average net worth and income levels. Despite stubborn inflation and tariff woes, TOL has some pricing flexibility to offset cost pressures.

2. Its business has a wide range of geographical distribution. Toll Brothers, Inc. is present across over 20 states in the US. This helps it maintain strong brand popularity and reputation. This also allows it to segment prices based on their respective market conditions. This also shows that TOL picks the right market or states in line with the products it offers. In short, it has a high concentration on states with the highest number of HNWIs or those states with high-income levels per household. It also prioritizes states that are home to big corporations. After all, it won’t succeed in states whose individuals prioritize more affordable housing. It also won't thrive in those with unfavorable terrains.

For instance, its average price in North rose by 10.4% as demand. On average, this has the second-highest unit price among its five markets. This should not be surprising since it caters to Massachusetts, New York, and New Jersey, which are among the states with the highest per capita personal income. With respect to median income, these three states are within the top 15. The remaining states are within the middle, while Michigan is on the lower end. The same goes for its Mountain market with a higher average price and number of units.

In other markets, TOL had to lower its pricing either to boost demand or balance price with volume to stabilize their revenues. As we can see, its Pacific Market is the most expensive on the list. This should not be surprising since California is one of the richest states in the US. Meanwhile, Washington and Oregon are still on the upper half with respect to per capita and median income. It doesn’t cater to any state in the region that ranks 25th and below. In addition, its demand improved amid lower prices.

Overall, its operations remain strong as the total units rose by 145 and the average price still increased to $973,600 versus $968,200 YoY. With that, it’s not surprising that its operating revenue improved by 8.0% from $2.7B to $2.9B YoY. It was also able to stabilize margins and cost sensitivity through operating leverage.

3. TOL enjoys strong pricing power. In connection with numbers 1 and 2, TOL still enjoys pricing flexibility as demand remains robust. Although contracts decreased, TOL was still able to raise its average price. Its North market remains the most resilient among the group. In Mid-Atlantic, its lower pricing bolstered volume. Meanwhile, pricing completely offsets volume in the Pacific market. This can be supported by the decreasing mortgage rate to 6.2% and the recent Fed rate cut of 25 bps. While HNWIs may not be affected that much, these economic indicators may help improve confidence among them. After all, luxury homes are not solely for personal use. They can be good investments due to their higher appreciation potential, tax benefits, and rental income stability.

4. Luxury Market Balances Demand With Supply. We can see the notable decrease in contracts and backlogs of Toll Brothers, Inc., and I think the whole market is on the same page. That way, players can improve inventory management and maintain their pricing power. This is why TOL still enjoys a higher average price in deliveries, contracts, and backlogs on a YoY basis, allowing it to offset lower volumes.

On a broader scale, we can see how new listings of single-family and attached homes started to moderate amid the higher levels of inventory. This is how the market tries to balance new listings versus total sold units. This remains effective as their median list and sale prices rose last August on a YoY basis.

With the combined impact of numbers 1, 2, 3, and 4, I believe that TOL’s Q4 and 2025 guidance remains realistic at this point. The target price range of $950,000 to $960,000 is slightly higher than the nine-month average of $946,400. If we use these prices and multiply them by the total deliveries of 11,200, we can derive its FY 2025 revenue from $10,640 to $10,750. We can also derive COGS based on the sales gross margin and SG&A as a percentage of revenue. To have a broader view, I also estimated depreciation and capex using the straight-line method. So, I can also compute NOPAT and EBITDA. As we can see, NOPAT and EBITDA will decrease in line with the previous three quarters. The consolation is that TOL will remain very profitable to sustain its operations.

5. Its fundamentals are still well-positioned and robust. With its profitable operations and effective management of operating assets and liabilities, TOL maintains enough operating cash flow for its capex.

It complements it with prudent debt management to ensure liquidity while covering dividends, debt repayments, and share repurchases. It also maintains an adequate capital buffer considering its low Net Debt/EBITDA of 1.0x. This means that TOL only needs a year to cover all debt at once, even if it doesn’t have to. This is in line with its maturity profile. For instance, its senior notes of $500M will mature in 2035. Meanwhile, its unsecured notes of $650M and revolving loans with a borrowing capacity of over $2B will mature in 2030. Hence, TOL has huge resources to ensure sustainability and dividend longevity.

Valuation

After the sharp stock price surge, I’m revisiting TOL. But this time, I opted to use more forward-looking methods.

First, I started with the Dividend Discount Model. Its annualized dividends of $1.00 are based on the quarterly payments of $0.25. Meanwhile, its dividend growth rate of 11.43% is based on the average growth rate in the past four years to exclude the pandemic year. Lastly, I got its cost of equity using the capital asset pricing model. Note that I set its risk premium at 6.0% to price in more risks. I computed its Beta on MS Excel using the SLOPE function of its daily price change versus the S&P 500. This is almost the same as the reported value of 1.33 on Yahoo Finance, proving the precision of my computation. Using all these numbers, its TP will be $140.71. This means that TOL is still trading at a reasonable level. However, its upside appears to be limited at this point. If I add a 5% allowance, the TP will be $133.39.

I also used EV/Revenue based on the average home price and unit delivery guidance I explained earlier. The EV/Revenue high and low will be 1.46x and 1.48x, so their midpoint is 1.47x. But just like the dividend discount model, the current multiple shows that TOL is still reasonably valued. Note that the TTM and FWD multiples are both 1.45x. With that, they have already priced in the target revenue of TOL. If we use this to derive the fair value, we will get $141.46. But still, its upside also appears limited. With that, I advise investors to stay on the sidelines for now.

Technicals

TOL’s momentum still demonstrates bullish signals as it trends upward with higher highs and lows. The 50-day SMA is still increasing above the 200-day SMA, suggesting that market sentiments are still generally optimistic. However, I advise investors to be more cautious as the 50-day SMA gets closer to the price. We should also note the price dip in the past two weeks, which reflects some weakness that the 50-day SMA may not have reflected yet.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI can explain it. We can see that the MACD line is moving below the signal line, showing the increasing selling volume and decreasing buying volume. Meanwhile, the histogram remains within negative territory, so selling volume still outpaces buying volume. Likewise, RSI has weakened as it reflected the dip. This is due to the recent overbuying that preceded profit-taking. Now, it starts to increase again in line with the price rebound attempts. This means that buyers saw the dip as an opportunity to find new support and enter.

Conclusion

Toll Brothers, Inc. remains resilient with its strategic business model and vast domestic presence that supports its pricing flexibility and top-line stability. It also keeps robust liquidity while taking advantage of new macroeconomic opportunities for luxury properties. Yet, entering at its current price appears challenging as it gets very close to my target price and limits upside potential. Technicals adhere as they present some weaknesses in the price trend even if the bullish trend still holds today. I advise investors to stay on the sidelines and downgrade my rating to hold for now.

data.title: Tradeweb Government Bond Update - September 2025
data.description:
Global government debt markets saw mixed yield movements in September, with both rises and declines recorded during the month. Beginning in the Eurozone, French government bond markets remained under pressure in September, as investors continued to reassess the country’s fiscal outlook.

The yield on France’s 10-year benchmark bond fell three basis points to 3.47% at month-end. On September 8, Prime Minister Francois Bayrou lost a confidence vote, and while the country has a new prime minister, Sébastien Lecornu, it is still without an official government. Meanwhile, the annual inflation rate rose to 1.2% in September from 0.9% the month before.

Roderick Joniaux, Head of European Government and Supranational Products at Tradeweb, said, "The 10-year OAT yield climbed to a three-year high of 3.61% on September 2, while the spread over the German 10-year Bund widened to 76 basis points at month-end – its widest level in three weeks. The move highlights persistent investor concerns around France’s elevated debt burden and recent credit rating downgrades, with Fitch lowering the sovereign to A+ from AA- and Morningstar DBRS cutting it to AA from AA (high). Against this backdrop, French bonds have become a focal point for gauging sentiment on Eurozone stability, with markets closely watching whether fiscal consolidation efforts can restore confidence in the months ahead.”

In neighbouring Germany, the 10-year Bund yield remained unchanged from August at 2.71%. Similar to its French counterpart, the country’s annual inflation rate rose from 2.2% in August to 2.4% in September. The HCOB Germany Manufacturing PMI for September was revised upward to 49.5 from the earlier estimate of 48.5, but slightly below August’s 49.8, signalling that manufacturing activity remained close to stagnation.

Inflation in the Eurozone rose to 2.2% in September, the first time it has gone above the European Central Bank’s target of 2% since April this year. On September 11, the central bank voted to hold interest rates steady at 2%.

The Italian 10-year government bond yield fell five basis points to 3.56% at the end of the month. According to preliminary estimates, the country’s annual inflation rate remained unchanged in September at 1.6%, slightly under market expectations.

The UK 10-year Gilt yield dropped just two basis points from August to 4.15% at the end of September. The country’s annual inflation rate stayed at 3.8% in August, matching July’s figure and remaining near the peak levels recorded in January 2024.

The Bank of England voted 7–2 to keep interest rates unchanged at 4% on September 18. The S&P Global UK Manufacturing PMI fell to 46.2 in September from 47 in the previous month, while the GfK Consumer Confidence Index fell to -19 from -17 in August.

Over in the U.S., the 10-year Treasury yield finished September at 4.15%, seven basis points lower than the month prior. On September 17, the Federal Reserve lowered borrowing costs for the first time since December last year to a range of 4% to 4.25%. The country’s annual inflation rate accelerated to 2.9% in September, the highest level since January 2025, after holding steady at 2.7% in June and July.

Finally, in Asia Pacific, the yield on Japan’s 10-year government bond rose four basis points to 1.65% at month-end. The benchmark bond also reached a three-year yield high of 1.66% on September 22. On September 19, the Bank of Japan announced it was maintaining interest rates at 0.75%. The country’s annual inflation rate eased once again to 2.7% in August from 3.1% in the previous month.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: China's Flow-Driven Equity Rally May Have Further Upside
data.description:
By Han Peng, CFA, Director, Multi-Asset Research and Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist

Despite mounting macro headwinds at the start of the year, Chinese equities have continued to deliver positive performance. Fueling the rally has been a consumption-driven stimulus approach from domestic policymakers, which has helped restore retail investor confidence.

While near-term flows can provide further upside, the durability for the equity rally will hinge on policy choices ahead. A sustained bull market will require more than just liquidity, but a measured stimulus approach that limits leverage-driven excesses.

The Shanghai Composite Index reached a ten-year high in August, fueled by ongoing stimulus efforts from policymakers in China that have helped stabilize growth concerns and revive investor confidence.

While the extended U.S.-China trade war truce has helped ease concerns, the response from domestic policymakers has been a significant positive catalyst. Contrary to the old “bazooka stimulus” playbook aimed at investment, policymakers have instead adopted a more managed approach focused on consumption.

Thus far, this has included a consumption trade-in program, a birth subsidy, and a consumer loan interest subsidy. As a result, consensus forecasts for China’s 2025 GDP growth have rebounded, helping revive domestic investor confidence, especially among retail investors.

Recent deposit data has shown increased rotation out of household bank deposits (savings) into non-bank financial institutions - a proxy for increased market participation. Looking ahead, these retail flows could provide further momentum for the equity rally, and with bank deposits still elevated, the rebalancing is likely to have further to run.

With retail flows potentially driving equity market upside from here, the pace of the rally becomes increasingly important. While a repeat of the 2015 rally, which was largely driven by leverage, would risk instability, to the extent that Chinese policy makers can continue to adopt a balanced approach with stimulus and prevent excesses from forming, a healthy and long-lasting bull market can emerge.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Largest U.S. Banks Lead Sector Market Performance In September
data.description:
Although most of the US banking sector traded down in September, the Big Four banks bucked the trend, propping up the market-cap weighted index.

Each of the four US banks with more than $1 trillion in total assets as of June 30 posted positive total returns in September. Citigroup Inc. (C) led the way, trading up 5.1%. JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), Bank of America Corp. (BAC) and Wells Fargo & Co. (WFC) followed with returns of 4.6%, 2.2% and 2.0%, respectively.

Those positive returns did not extend to the next largest asset tier. Eight of the 10 banks with assets between $100 billion and $1 trillion recorded negative returns; Citizens Financial Group Inc. (CFG) traded up 1.7% and U.S. Bancorp (USB) ticked up a fraction of a percent.

Banks between $50 billion and $100 billion in assets fared a bit better. Six of the 19 stocks went up in September, highlighted by Valley National Bancorp's (VLY) 2.4% return. Of the remaining 175 banks in an S&P Global Market Intelligence analysis, 16 experienced a positive total return in September.

For all 208 banks in the analysis, the median return in September was negative 3.4%, which trailed the 1.6% return of the S&P US BMI Banks index and the 3.7% return of the S&P 500. Market pressure pushed down valuations. The median price-to-adjusted tangible book value (TBV) for the industry was 141.9% as of Sept. 30, down from 145.4% at the end of August.

Least expensive banks

Dallas-based First Foundation Inc. (FFWM) was the least expensive bank in the analysis by price-to-adjusted TBV for the ninth month in a row. Its valuation was 50.5% at the end of September and would have been higher if preferred shares from a capital raise in July 2024 were converted to common stock. Such a conversion would have dropped the bank's basic TBV to $9.34 per common share from $11.65 per common share as of June 30, according to a July 31 filing.

First Internet Bancorp (INBK), which had the second-lowest price-to-adjusted TBV, was the fourth-weakest market performer in the analysis in September, with a total return of negative 10.5%. The bank expects to report a material impairment charge in the third quarter from the sale of performing single-tenant lease financing loans in September.

The No. 15 bank, OceanFirst Financial Corp. (OCFC), plans to pursue organic growth rather than M&A. The Toms River, New Jersey-based bank has been hiring bankers who can attract low-cost deposits.

Most expensive banks

The Bancorp Inc. (TBBK) screened as the highest-valued bank for the third consecutive month. Its price-to-adjusted TBV was 438.5% as of Sept. 30, down 8 percentage points from Aug. 29, but more than 100 percentage points higher than any other bank in the analysis.

The second-most expensive bank — Dewitt, New York-based Community Financial System Inc. (CBU) — announced a minority investment in Leap Holdings Inc. in September.

Triumph Financial Inc. (TFIN), the No. 8 bank by highest valuation, disclosed its exposure to subprime auto lender Tricolor Holdings LLC, which filed for Chapter 7 bankruptcy on Sept. 10. Triumph had the worst monthly return in the analysis at negative 18.6%.

Also in September, Nicolet Bankshares Inc. (NIC), the 18th-most expensive bank, announced leadership continuity through 2030. The Green Bay, Wisconsin-based bank has earned a return on average tangible common equity in excess of 17% for the last seven quarters.

About this analysis

S&P Global Market Intelligence analyzed US banks trading on the Nasdaq, NYSE or NYSE American with total assets of more than $3 billion. The analysis excludes banks in the mutual holding company ownership structure and other operating subsidiaries.

Adjusted tangible book value is calculated as the sum of tangible common equity, loss reserves and unrealized gain or loss from held-to-maturity securities, tax-adjusted at the 21% corporate rate, less nonperforming assets and loans 90 or more days past due but still accruing interest, divided by common shares outstanding.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Weekly Indicators: Almost Completely Unaffected By Government Shutdown
data.description:
Purpose

I look at the high-frequency weekly indicators because while they can be very noisy, they provide a good nowcast of the economy, and will telegraph the maintenance or change in the economy well before monthly or quarterly data is available. They are also an excellent way to "mark your beliefs to market." In general, I go in order of long leading indicators, then short leading indicators, then coincident indicators.

A Note on Methodology

Data is presented in a "just the facts, ma'am" format with a minimum of commentary so that bias is minimized.

Where relevant, I include 12-month highs and lows in the data in parentheses to the right. All data taken from St. Louis FRED unless otherwise linked.

A few items (e.g., Financial Conditions indexes, regional Fed indexes, stock prices, the yield curve) have their own metrics based on long-term studies of their behavior.

Where data is seasonally adjusted, generally it is scored positively if it is within the top 1/3 of that range, negative in the bottom 1/3, and neutral in between. Where it is not seasonally adjusted, and there are seasonal issues, waiting for the YoY change to change sign will lag the turning point. Thus I make use of a convention: data is scored neutral if it is less than 1/2 as positive/negative as at its 12-month extreme.

With long leading indicators, which by definition turn at least 12 months before a turning point in the economy as a whole, there is an additional rule: data is automatically negative if, during an expansion, it has not made a new peak in the past year, with the sole exception that it is scored neutral if it is moving in the right direction and is close to making a new high.

For all series where a graph is available, I have provided a link to where the relevant graph can be found.

Recap of Monthly Reports

September data included contractionary readings in the ISM manufacturing index as well as its new orders and employment indexes, while the ISM services index was exactly flat, while its new orders was slightly expansionary, but its employment index was also contractionary.

August data included manufacturers new durable goods orders, core capital goods orders, and consumer durable goods new orders, all of which increased. House prices as measured by both the Case Shiller and FHFA Indexes decreased again. JOLTS data included an increase in openings, but declines in both hires and quits, and an increase in layoffs.

Long leading indicators

Interest rates and credit spreads

Rates

  • BAA corporate bond index 5.78%, down -0.07% w/w (1-yr range: 5.47-6.43)
  • 10-year Treasury bonds 4.12%, down -0.06% w/w (3.66-4.98)
  • Credit spread 1.66%, down -0.01% w/w (1.36-2.02)

(Graph at Moody's Seasoned Baa Corporate Bond Yield | FRED | St. Louis Fed )

Yield curve

  • 10 year minus 2 year: +0.55%, up +0.02% w/w (-1.07 - +0.55) (new 12 month high)
  • 10 year minus 3 month: +0.16%, down -0.06% w/w (-1.65 - 0.17)
  • 2 year minus Fed funds: -0.52%, down -0.08% w/w 3.56-4.33

(Graph at 10-Year Treasury Constant Maturity Minus 2-Year Treasury Constant Maturity | FRED | St. Louis Fed )

30-Year conventional mortgage rate (from Mortgage News Daily)

  • 6.34%, down -.04% w/w (6.13-7.26)

Treasury and corporate bond yields, as well as mortgage rates, remain lower than their near 12 month highs of a number of months ago, and declined further after the poor August jobs report. The spreads between Treasuries and investment grade and high yield bonds continue to show little change, having meandered in a relatively narrow range for most of the past several years.

The yield curve steepened between short and long rates after the Fed decision, but otherwise remains where it has been, more or less, for months, with the 10-2 year spread being the most positive, and the 2 year vs. Fed funds being the most negative. The 10 year minus 3 month retreated to neutral this week. It is particularly interesting that the 2 year vs. Fed funds spread continues to be very negative.

Housing

Mortgage applications (from the Mortgage Bankers Association)

  • Purchase apps down -1% to 161 (114-163) [SA]
  • Purchase apps 4 wk avg. up +4 to 161 [SA]
  • Purchase apps YoY% up +16% [NSA]
  • Purchase apps YoY 4 wk avg. +19% [NSA]
  • Refi apps down -21% w/w [SA]
  • Refi apps YoY up +17% [SA]

*[SA] = seasonally adjusted, [NSA] = not seasonally adjusted

Real Estate Loans (from the FRB)

  • Up less than +0.1% w/w
  • Up +1.4% YoY

(Graph at Real Estate Loans, All Commercial Banks | FRED | St. Louis Fed )

Both purchase mortgage and refinancing applications have been volatile, but within a limited range over the past 18 months. Both are now substantially higher YoY, but remain close to their longer term bottoms, so they generally score neutral.

In 2023 real estate loans sank below 1/2 of their 12 month high, the last housing indicator to turn negative, and have generally continued to worsen. This week they made yet another multi-year low.

Money supply

The Federal Reserve has discontinued this weekly series. Data is now only released monthly. August data was just released last week:

  • M1 m/m up +0.2%, YoY Real M1 up +1.5% ( -1.4% 8/23 - +1.8% 7/25)
  • M2 m/m up +0.4%, YoY Real M2 up +1.8% (2.1% June 2025 3 year high) (worst -8.6% 9/23)

No recession has happened without a YoY real M1 negative, or YoY real M2 below +2.5%. Real M2 fell below that threshold in March 2022. Real M1 also turned negative as of May 2022. In the past, when real money supply has rebounded to the levels it is at now, it has either been during the latter part of a recession, or in the period of recovery, so both of these are positive long leading metrics now.

Corporate profits (Q2 actual, Q3 estimated) from I/B/E/S via FactSet at p. 33)

  • Q2 unchanged at 67.03, up +5.3% q/q
  • Q3 down -0.07 to 67.46, up +0.6% q/q

FactSet estimates earnings, which are replaced by actual earnings as they are reported, and are updated weekly. The "neutral" band is +/-3%. I also average the previous two quarters together, until at least 100 companies have actually reported. In Q1 earnings wound up lower by more than -3%, which turned the rating negative. But in Q2 earnings rebounded sharply, causing the rating change to positive.

Credit conditions (from the Chicago Fed)

  • Financial Conditions Index up +.01 ( looser ) to -0.56 (-0.42 - -0.67)
  • Adjusted Index (removing background economic conditions) up +.01 (looser) to -0.52(-0.34 - -0.61)
  • Leverage subindex up +0.05 (less loose) to -0.28 (+0.13 - -0.76)

In these indexes, lower = better for the economy. The Chicago Fed's Adjusted Index's real break-even point is roughly -0.25. In the leverage index, a negative number is good, a positive poor. The historical breakeven point has been -0.5 for the unadjusted Index. After briefly deteriorating to neutral several months ago, all three had been solidly positive, but the unadjusted index remains neutral.

Short leading indicators

Economic Indicators from the late Jeff Miller’s “Weighing the Week Ahead”

  • Miller Score (formerly "C-Score"): up +13 to 380, up +3 m/m (288 9/27/24 - 421 7/25/25) Components: Yield curve 64 Phillips curve 316
  • St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index: up +0.00567 to -0.5340 (+0.5569 4/11/25 - -.9751 1/24/25)
  • BCIp from Georg Vrba: 57.5 as of 9/11/25 iM's Business Cycle Index (100 is max value, below 25 is recession signal averaging 20 weeks ahead)

The Miller Score is designed to look 52 weeks ahead for whether or not a recession is possible. Any score over 500 means no recession. This number fell below that threshold at the beginning of August 2021, so not only is it negative, but we are now years into the “recession eligible” time period. This indicator is heavily influenced by both the yield curve and the still very low unemployment rate vs. gas prices (expecting a reversal) which explains why it remains negative. My colleague who compiles this score began disaggregating the two components several months ago. Gas prices and yield curve gyrations remain the most volatile inputs.

The St. Louis Financial Stress index is one where a negative score is a positive for the economy, and during its limited existence, has risen above zero before a recession by less than one year. It remained very positive until briefly in April. It then recovered, and until last week remained among its better post-pandemic readings.

For 18 months, IM has stated that their system does not forecast a recession. Recently it did edge closer to their threshold, but has since rebounded.

Trade-weighted US$

  • Up +0.64 to 120.86 w/w, down -0.4% YoY (last week) (broad) (119.67 - 130.21) (Graph at Nominal Broad U.S. Dollar Index
  • Down -0.45 to 97.71 w/w, down -4.7% YoY (major currencies) (graph at link) (96.22- 110.18)

The neutral range for the US$ is between unchanged to 5% higher YoY. At 5% higher or more YoY, the strength suppresses exports and incentivizes imports, a negative for the economy. The trend in these measures had been from below 0 (thus positive) to neutral, to above 5% (negative) for 18 months, until the beginning of this year. Both measures are lower YoY now, so are positives.

I mechanically score a slowly depreciating US$ as a positive because it makes exports cheaper and imports more expensive, thereby assisting domestic production. If it is more a sign of relative US weakness - and given the behavior of precious metals, I suspect it is - then it would warrant a different conclusion.

Commodity prices

Bloomberg Commodity Index

  • Up +0.25 to 105.35 (95.97 - 107.71)
  • Up +3.2% YoY

(Graph at https://www.marketwatch.com/investing/index/bcom?countrycode=xx )

Bloomberg Industrial metals ETF (from Bloomberg) (graph at link)

  • 151.54, up +6.23 w/w (130.47 -160.83 )
  • Down -4.4% YoY

This spring the industrial measure gradually weakened from positive to negative. Then the broad commodity index declined sharply into negative territory, but then rebounded into neutral range and now positive. By contrast, the industrial metals measure remained very negative. This can be attributed to whether or not oil is included among the commodities tracked. Last week the industrial measure turned back higher YoY, and so positive. Please note, importantly, that if commodities were not priced in US$ terms, but in other major currencies, it is a completely different story. In particular, that industrial commodities are weaker YoY, despite a weaker US$, is definitely not a sign of global strength. Additionally, BCOM includes precious metals (gold and silver), without which it would be neutral, and in non-US$ terms is sharply negative.

Stock prices S&P 500 (from CNBC) (graph at link)

  • Up +1.1% to 6715.79

Since July stocks have made repeated new all-time highs. As a result, this indicator is positive.

Regional Fed New Orders Indexes

(*indicates report this week)

  • Empire State https://www.newyorkfed.org/survey/empire/empiresurvey_overview.html up +4.5 to +19.9
  • Philly up +14.3 to +12.4
  • Richmond down -9 to -15
  • Kansas City down -3 to +2
  • *Dallas down -8.4 to -2.6
  • Month-over-month rolling average: down -2 to +3

The regional average is more volatile than the ISM manufacturing index, but usually correctly forecasts its month-over-month direction. Since spring 2022, these gradually declined to neutral and then negative. In the past two months, these have improved to neutral and now to positive. In September they have softened somewhat.

Since May I have added the regional service indexes. Those reports posted in the past week noted with an *:

  • Empire State down -7.7 to -19.4
  • Philly up +14.4 to +24.6
  • Richmond down -13 to -7
  • Kansas City down -13 to -9
  • *Dallas down -12.4 to -5.6
  • Month over month rolling average: down -3 to -3

The most positive recent trend in all of the high frequency data in August was the sharp rebound in manufacturing all the way to solidly positive. Meanwhile the regional non-manufacturing reports, which had stabilized at negative levels since May, rebounded all the way to neutral, and two weeks ago to positive. Since then they have retreated back to negative.

Employment metrics

Initial jobless claims (NOT AVAILABLE DUE TO GOVERNMENT SHUTDOWN)

  • 218,000, down -14,000 w/w, down -1.4% YoY (last week)
  • 4-week average 237,500, down -2,750 w/w, up 5.4% YoY (last week)

(Graph at St. Louis FRED)

Jobless claims were positive for most of 2024, except for hurricane-influenced weeks during the autumn. Then they turned moderately negative through midyear this year. But in July and August this indicator improved to positive before retreating in September.

This metric was reported during the last several government shutdowns, perhaps because it simply involved adding up all the States’ reports and then applying a seasonal factor. Many States do report their own individual numbers, but these were not available in time for this week’s report.

Temporary staffing index (from the American Staffing Association) (graph at link) *****

  • Up +1 to 90 w/w
  • Up +0.2% YoY (low -13.6%- high +0.2% this week)

This series has been negative since early 2023. YoY comparisons continued to be negative throughout 2024. It remains frankly recessionary. I suspect this has been a secular change and giving a false signal as a result. Relatively speaking, the YoY reading has been trending “less bad” for the past number of months, and finally, since it is now more than half the way to unchanged on a YoY basis, to neutral as of earlier this year.

Tax Withholding (from the Department of the Treasury)

  • $292.2 B for the month of September this year vs. $277.3 B last year, up +$14.9 B or +5.4%
  • $261.9 B for the last 20 reporting days vs. $259.6 B last year, up +$2.3 B or +0.9%

These were very positive for almost all of 2024, and with the exception of seven weeks (four after an April 15 anomaly), the trend has continued this year. This week it was again mildly positive.

Oil prices and usage (from the E.I.A.)

  • Oil down -$4.63 to $60.69 w/w, down -13.2% YoY ($58.21 - $83.88)
  • Gas prices down -$.05 $3.12 w/w, down -$0.06 YoY
  • Usage 4-week average up +0.5% YoY

(Information can be found at “This Week In Petroleum” at the E.I.A.’s website)

Oil prices first declined into the bottom 1/3rd of their 3 year range, and so turned positive late in 2024, and then declined even further earlier this year. After the Israeli-Iran hostilities last month, oil prices increased, but have since receded back to close to four year lows.

Gas prices also recently made new 3 year lows, and so are also positive. Last year, mileage driven had been generally negative. It turned neutral and then positive earlier this year, but negative again last week. Since March of this year it has oscillated between positive and negative. In general both gas prices and usage have approached neutrality.

Note: given this measure’s extreme volatility, I believe the best measure is against their 3 year average.

Bank lending rates

  • 4.20% Secured Overnight Financing Rate (SOFR), up +0.02 w/w

The TED Spread has been discontinued, and as of last September so was LIBOR. At the suggestion of a reader, I began to track the SOFR instead. Unfortunately, SOFR has only been in existence since 2018, so there is no track record as to how it might behave around normal recessions (vs. the pandemic). Over the past 5 years, it does appear to have matched the trend in LIBOR. Given the alternatives, I am now including it in my list of indicators in the conclusion.

Coincident indicators

Tariff payments (from the Department of the Treasury)

  • August 2025 vs. August 2024: $31.4 B vs. $8.9 B (+$22.5 B)
  • September 2025 vs. September 2024: $31.7 B vs. $8.8 B (+$22.9 B)

I have inaugurated this measure temporarily since other sources, such as inferences from the CPI and PPI, are imprecise and speculative so far. The above information comes directly from the Daily Treasury Statement. These fees are either absorbed by the importer or passed on to the consumer, or some proportion of each, and are removed from potential consumption on other items. Note that some tariff hikes that went into effect last month.

St. Louis FRED Weekly Economic Index

  • Up +0.35 to 2.40 w/w (Low 1.65 November 2, 2024 - high 3.21 November 23, 2024)

This measure was in the neutral range during most of early 2024, but generally positive (above 2.00) since then, and came very close to setting a new 12 month high three months ago, before declining. After four weeks back in neutral range, including a new 6 month low, early this year it returned to positive territory and has remained there since.

Restaurant reservations YoY (from Open Table) State of the Restaurant Industry |

  • Last week - 7 day average up +11% YoY, vs. +6% one week prior

Since 2023 this index has been very volatile, swinging between positive, neutral, and negative several times. In general earlier this year the YoY trends improved, but with a great deal of noise. In summer this metric trended sharply higher, before backing off somewhat in the previous five weeks, and improving this week.

Consumer spending

  • Johnson Redbook up +5.9% YoY, 4 week average +6.1% (high 7.4% 4/25/25; 4.8% 2/1 and 7/3, 2025) United States Redbook Index

The Redbook index has been positive since February of last year. It had very good readings in April and early May, probably due to consumers front-running anticipated tariff price increases and shortages. Afterward it did weaken of a month, but four weeks ago it increased sharply, and has remained very positive since. This presumably means that the period of backlash from previous front-running of the tariffs has ended. I suspect that the “wealth effect” from the sharp increase in stock prices is crucial to this trend. In the past three weeks it has backed off somewhat but still remains very positive.

Consumer inflation by Truflation (Independent, economic & financial data in real time on-chain | Truflation)

  • Up +0.21% to +2.25 YoY (High 3.11% 12/11/24 - Low 1.20% 4/8/25)

This recent addition is a daily update to inflation, similar to the “billion prices project” of the last decade (which required a subscription). I have not added this to my list below of coincident or leading indicators, but needless to say it is an up-to-the-moment reading on this very important indicator. This series has been volatile, and the YoY comparisons had been turning higher since September, but has been very low in the last several months, although it did make another new two month high four months ago.

Real Consumer Spending

  • Up +3.7% YoY (high - 6.0% 4/25/25; 12 month low 1.7% 12/16/24)

This metric premiered at the beginning of last year. One of my most important mantras is that consumption leads employment. Real retail sales have a long history of doing so, but are only reported on a monthly basis.

The weekly result is derived simply by subtracting YoY inflation as measured by Truflation from the YoY change in nominal consumer spending as measured by Redbook. While it will be somewhat noisy, it should anticipate changes in the monthly measures ahead of time. It backed off from the 12 month high it set in February, and then gradually rebounded, and made another 12 month high several months ago. After mainly weeks in the upper part of its 12 month range, it was tepid at the beginning of the year, before improving in the past two months, even making a new 12 month high before backing off. It has improved considerably along with nominal consumer spending in the past several months.

Transport

Railroads (from the AAR)

  • Carloads up +0.9% YoY (cumulative 2025 vs. 2024 up +2.1%)
  • Intermodal units up +1.1% YoY (cumulative up +3.5%)
  • Total loads up +1.0% YoY (cumulative up +2.9%)

(Graph at Railfax Report - North American Rail Freight Traffic Carloading Report )

Shipping transport

  • Harpex down -9 to 2203
  • Baltic Dry Index down -286 to 1980

Rail was mixed for many weeks before turning positive in March, presumably reflecting businesses attempting to get inventory before the new tariffs kick in. Intermodal rail loads had steadily decelerated since then, and five weeks ago turned negative. The total number of total loads was negative for several weeks as well. All three measures improved sharply in July, and remained positive, but weakened in August, and are negative again last week. This week it rebounded to positive again.

Harpex was as high as nearly 4500 almost 2 years ago, but declined as low as 810 in December of last year before rising, setting repeated 12 month highs in the past four months. The usual interpretation for this is that demand is straining against supply, showing a strong global economy, and thus very positive.

Similarly, the BDI was in a generally declining trend throughout 2022 before bottoming at a three year low of 530 in February 2023. The overall trend has been higher since then, rising to a high of 3346 last December, probably reflecting the Houthi attacks on shipping in the Red Sea. This year its low was 1309 in January, and its recent high was 2419 last March. It declined to the bottom 1/3rd of its one year range during the rest of the year, but rebounded all the way to the top of its six month range earlier this year, before retreating back into the middle of that range.

I am wary of reading too much into price indexes like this, since they are heavily influenced by supply (as in, a huge overbuilding of ships in the last decade) as well as demand.

Steel production (American Iron and Steel Institute)

  • Up +0.9% w/w
  • Up +6.8% YoY

This metric has been very volatile for over a year. Recently it improved to neutral and then positive for two weeks before backing off to neutral again in May. Last week its YoY measure was sufficiently small to change the rating to neutral, and eight weeks ago it returned to positive.

.

As per the title this week, one of the advantages of the high frequency indicators I track in this article is that they are not dependent on the functioning of federal agencies. Only one indicator - jobless claims - was unavailable; and if the shutdown drags on I can probably post an estimate based on the States’ individual reports with a one week delay, as the first of those was only posted Friday afternoon.

Let me also reiterate that the commodity prices suggest, contrary to the normal take, that the global economy is weak, but the US$ is even weaker.

Turning to the long leading indicators, the story continues to be that yield curve spreads in the long leading indicators continue to be more positive except for the short end comparisons. Corporate profit growth is also a plus. Meanwhile, housing, on the other hand, is frankly recessionary.

The mechanistic readings of both short leading and coincident indicators are that they are very positive. But the regional Fed services surveys turning negative is a very big caution, mirroring the poor ISM services report on Friday. I continue to believe that consumer spending (which may be being fueled by a wealth effect from stock market gains) remains key to the near term forecast.
